skill.name: context-persistence
skill.description: Helps AI coding assistants preserve task context across long-running work by maintaining waypoint plans, progress notes, task checklists, and resume instructions.
skill.summary: Preserve project context across coding sessions with structured waypoint plans.
skill.author: DojoCodingLabs

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found documentation examples rather than executable code. No prompt injection, credential access, malicious networking, or real command execution was found in SKILL.md.

low_findings.title: Markdown Command Examples Misclassified as Execution
low_findings.description: The external command detections point to inline Markdown paths, fenced examples, and documented slash commands. They do not contain Ruby code, shell backticks, or executable command interpolation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are visible Markdown documentation and plugin command examples. I found no executable Ruby, shell backticks, or user-controlled command construction.

low_findings.title: Security Terms Misclassified as Sensitive Access
low_findings.description: The browser credential, weak cryptography, system reconnaissance, and network reconnaissance detections are caused by ordinary text in planning examples. The file does not read browser files, implement cryptography, or probe systems or networks.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding context is an example authentication plan and checklist language. No evidence found of browser credential access, cryptographic implementation, host discovery, or network scanning.

content.value_statement: Long coding tasks lose momentum when context resets or sessions end. This skill provides a repeatable waypoint system for plans, progress, tasks, and fast resumption.
actual_capabilities: Defines a three-file waypoint structure for plans, context, and task checklists.
Shows how to track session progress with completed, in-progress, pending, blocked, and next-action sections.
Provides quick resume instructions for interrupted or reset coding sessions.
Explains when to create waypoint plans for multi-session or complex work.
Documents naming conventions and directory layout for active and archived plans.
Includes examples for create-plan, update-context, resume, and archive workflows.
limitations: It is guidance documentation, not an executable persistence service.
It depends on users or commands keeping context files current.
It does not automatically validate project state after code changes.
It may be too heavy for trivial fixes or one-session tasks.

use_cases.title: Continue Multi-Session Features
use_cases.description: Keep a clear record of current files, decisions, blockers, and next actions while a feature spans several sessions.

use_cases.title: Prepare Team Handoffs
use_cases.description: Create structured notes that let another developer or AI assistant resume work with minimal explanation.

use_cases.title: Recover After Context Resets
use_cases.description: Use quick resume sections and task checklists to restart work after conversation history is lost.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Waypoint Plan
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a new task
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a waypoint plan for this feature. Include a plan file, context file, and task checklist using the recommended structure.

prompt_templates.title: Update Current Progress
prompt_templates.prompt: Update the session progress for this task. Capture what is complete, what is in progress, blockers, and the next action.

prompt_templates.title: Resume Stale Work
prompt_templates.prompt: Read the waypoint context and task checklist, verify whether the context is stale, and tell me the safest next action.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare a Handoff
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert the current work into a handoff-ready waypoint. Include decisions, modified files, unresolved risks, and precise resume steps.

output_examples.input: Create a waypoint for a payments integration that will take several sessions.
output_examples.output: A structured plan with overview, requirements, phases, dependencies, and risks, plus matching context and task checklist sections.

output_examples.input: Update context after finishing the auth middleware and starting token refresh.
output_examples.output: A session progress update showing completed middleware work, token refresh as current work, pending tests, and the next file to edit.

output_examples.input: Resume the dashboard refactor from last week.
output_examples.output: A concise resume summary with last known progress, stale-context checks, current blockers, and a recommended next action.

best_practices: Update context after meaningful milestones, decisions, blockers, or task switches.
Write next actions with exact files, functions, and expected follow-up steps.
Archive completed waypoint plans so active work remains easy to scan.
anti_patterns: Do not use waypoint plans for trivial one-line fixes.
Do not leave session progress stale after major code or decision changes.
Do not store credentials, API keys, or private customer data in context files.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps preserve task context so coding work can resume after interruptions, context resets, or handoffs.

faq.question: Does it run commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. The skill describes command workflows and documentation patterns, but SKILL.md itself is not executable code.

faq.question: When should I create a waypoint plan?
faq.answer: Create one for complex work, multi-session tasks, team handoffs, or features that may be interrupted.

faq.question: What files does the method use?
faq.answer: It uses a plan file for strategy, a context file for current state, and a tasks file for actionable checklist items.

faq.question: Can Codex use this workflow?
faq.answer: Yes. The structure is useful for Codex, Claude, and Claude Code when they need durable task context.

faq.question: What should not be written into context files?
faq.answer: Do not include credentials, API keys, private tokens, or sensitive customer data in waypoint documentation.
